label: ai
topic: A Chinese-American adoptee's relationship with dumplings as identity
text:
The first dumplings I remember came from a freezer bag, the plastic cloudy with frost, the instructions printed in English and something I couldn't yet read. My mother boiled them in a dented pot on our stove in Minnesota, the windows fogging up as the water rolled. When she lifted them out, they looked pale and swollen, their edges soft, like something unfinished. She set them on a plate and told me to wait, but I burned my tongue anyway, impatient.

At school, dumplings didn't exist. Lunch was peanut butter sandwiches and apple slices, the careful sameness of other kids' meals. Once, in second grade, I brought dumplings in a thermos. When I opened it, the smell escaped—warm, unfamiliar to everyone but me. A boy at my table wrinkled his nose and asked what it was. "It's like… a pocket," I said, struggling. "With stuff inside." I didn't bring them again for a long time.

We went to San Francisco's Chinatown when I was ten. It felt like stepping into a place that spoke a language my body almost recognized but couldn't quite translate. The streets were crowded, the signs layered in red and gold characters. In a small restaurant, we sat at a round table while carts rattled past, stacked with bamboo steamers. When the lid lifted, a cloud of steam rose, carrying the smell of something richer, deeper than what I knew. The dumplings here were different—translucent skins, pleated with care, each one precise. My mother watched me take a bite, her eyes searching my face. "These are closer," she said quietly. Closer to what, I didn't ask.

In high school, I learned to make them. Not well, not at first. My wrappers tore, my fillings spilled. I watched videos, read recipes, tried to mimic hands that moved with an ease I didn't have. The first time I got the fold right—just right, the pleats lining up neatly—I felt a small, unexpected pride. I brought a batch to a friend's house, setting them on the table like an offering. "These are amazing," she said, and I believed her, even as I knew they were still approximations.

At graduation, my parents took me out to dinner. Not to a Chinese restaurant, but to somewhere American, predictable. Afterward, at home, my mother made dumplings. We stood side by side in the kitchen, folding in silence. "When I was your age," she said, "I thought I had left all of this behind." She gestured to the dough, the filling, the rhythm of it. "But it doesn't leave you."

Years later, in Japan, I found dumplings again—gyoza this time, crisp on one side, delicate on the other. I sat in a small restaurant, listening to a language that wasn't mine but felt familiar in its distance. When I bit into one, the taste was different, but the shape was the same. A pocket, with stuff inside. I smiled at the memory.

Dumplings have never been just food. They are translation attempts, approximations of something that doesn't fully carry over. They are what I bring to the table when I'm not sure how to explain myself.

I still burn my tongue sometimes. I still rush the first bite.

Some things, I've learned, don't need to be perfect to be yours.
